summary: The strongest Qi tonic in Chinese medicine. Used for deep exhaustion, weak digestion, and recovery from illness.
actions: Strongly tonifies Qi and restores energy
Strengthens the Spleen and improves digestion
Nourishes the Lung and calms breathing
Calms the mind and improves focus
howToUse: Steep 2-3 thin slices in hot water for 10 minutes as tea. Or add to chicken soup and simmer for 2 hours.
dosage: 1-3g per day for general use. Up to 5g for deficiency.
cautions: Avoid during colds, flu, or fever
Do not combine with radish or strong tea
May cause insomnia if taken late at night
Not suitable for people with excess Heat

faqs.q: Can I take ginseng every day?
faqs.a: Small amounts (1-2 slices in tea) are generally safe for daily use by truly deficient types. Pause during colds or fever.

faqs.q: Does ginseng work as fast as caffeine?
faqs.a: No. Caffeine stimulates in minutes then crashes. Ginseng works gradually over weeks, rebuilding energy production.

faqs.q: Which body type benefits most from ginseng?
faqs.a: Qi Deficient and Yang Deficient types benefit most. Take the free body type quiz to check your type.

summary: A gentler Qi tonic than ginseng, often chosen for people who catch colds easily or have weak digestion. Good for daily use.
actions: Tonifies Spleen Qi and lifts energy
Strengthens the body defensive energy (Wei Qi)
howToUse: Simmer 5-10g in soup for 1-2 hours. Often added to chicken soup or bone broth.
dosage: 5-10g in food. Up to 15g for deficiency.
cautions: Avoid during acute colds or infections
Not suitable for Yin Deficiency with heat signs
May worsen bloating if digestion is very weak
Stop if you develop a fever

faqs.q: Is astragalus safer than ginseng?
faqs.a: Generally yes. Astragalus is gentler and more suitable for daily use, especially for people who catch colds easily.

faqs.q: How long until I feel the effects?
faqs.a: Most people notice steadier energy and fewer colds after 2-3 weeks of consistent use in soups or tea.

faqs.q: Can I use astragalus if I am always tired?
faqs.a: Yes, if your tiredness comes from Qi deficiency. Take the free body type quiz to confirm.

summary: The most commonly used food-herb in Chinese medicine. Sweet, nourishing, and gentle enough for daily use. Builds Qi and Blood, calms the mind.
actions: Tonifies Spleen Qi and strengthens digestion
Calms the mind and supports sleep
howToUse: Simmer 5-6 pitted dates in water for 15 minutes as tea. Add to congee, soup, or oatmeal.
dosage: 5-10 dates daily. Up to 15 for deficiency.
cautions: Avoid if you have dampness or bloating
Too many may cause sluggish digestion
Remove pits before cooking for better effect
Not suitable for Damp Heat patterns

faqs.q: Can I eat red dates every day?
faqs.a: Yes, 5-8 dates daily is safe for most people. They are gentle enough for daily use.

faqs.q: Are red dates the same as Middle Eastern dates?
faqs.a: No. Chinese red dates (jujube) are a different fruit, used specifically in Chinese medicine for building Qi and Blood.

faqs.q: Do red dates help with sleep?
faqs.a: Yes, especially when brewed as tea and drunk 30 minutes before bed. They calm the mind through nourishing the Heart.

summary: Small sweet red berries that nourish Liver and Kidney Yin. Popular worldwide for eye health, anti-aging, and gentle energy support.
howToUse: Eat 10-15 berries as a snack. Steep in hot water as tea. Add to oatmeal, congee, or soup.
cautions: Avoid during acute diarrhea
Not suitable for Damp Heat patterns in excess
May cause mild stomach upset in large amounts
Stop if you develop a sore throat

faqs.q: Can I eat goji berries raw?
faqs.a: Yes, dried goji berries can be eaten raw as a snack. They can also be steeped in tea or cooked in soups.

faqs.q: Do goji berries help with eye strain?
faqs.a: In Chinese medicine, goji berries nourish Liver Yin which governs eye health. Many notice less eye strain with regular use.

faqs.q: Are goji berries the same as wolfberries?
faqs.a: Yes. Goji berries are also called wolfberries. They are the same fruit.

summary: The most widely used warming food in Chinese medicine. Warms digestion, reduces nausea, and supports the body during colds.
actions: Releases cold from the body surface
howToUse: Slice 3-4 thin pieces and steep in hot water for 5 minutes. Add to soups, stir-fries, and congee.
dosage: 3-10g fresh daily. Dried ginger is stronger and used differently.
cautions: Avoid if you have internal Heat or Yin Deficiency
Do not use large amounts at night
Not suitable for people with hot flashes or night sweats
Reduce if you experience dry mouth

faqs.q: Is dried ginger the same as fresh ginger?
faqs.a: No. Fresh ginger warms the surface and releases cold. Dried ginger warms the interior more strongly.

faqs.q: Can ginger help with morning sickness?
faqs.a: Yes, ginger tea is one of the most effective natural remedies for nausea, including morning sickness.

faqs.q: Why should I avoid ginger at night?
faqs.a: Ginger is warming and energizing. Taking it late at night may interfere with sleep, especially for those who run warm.

summary: A delicate floral tea that cools internal heat, especially in the head and eyes. Caffeine-free and perfect for afternoon relaxation.
actions: Cools Liver heat and calms frustration
Benefits the eyes and reduces redness
howToUse: Steep 8-10 dried flowers in hot water for 5 minutes. Drink as tea, 2-3 cups daily.
cautions: Avoid if you have cold-type digestive issues
Not suitable for Yang Deficient types who run cold
May cause loose stools in excess
Use caution during pregnancy

faqs.q: Is chrysanthemum tea good for headaches?
faqs.a: Yes, especially headaches caused by stress, frustration, or Liver heat.

faqs.q: Can I drink chrysanthemum tea every day?
faqs.a: Yes, 2-3 cups daily is safe for most people, especially those who run warm or use screens extensively.

faqs.q: Does chrysanthemum tea have caffeine?
faqs.a: No, chrysanthemum tea is naturally caffeine-free.

summary: Tiny dark seeds that nourish the deepest energy reserves. Used for hair health, skin moisture, and gentle constipation relief.
actions: Benefits hair and skin from within
Moistens the intestines for gentle bowel movements
howToUse: Grind 2 tablespoons and mix with honey. Sprinkle on rice, salads, or toast.
cautions: Avoid if you have loose stools or diarrhea
Too much may cause greasy feeling or bloating
Best ground, not whole, for absorption
Store in a cool dry place to prevent rancidity

faqs.q: Do black sesame seeds help with hair loss?
faqs.a: In Chinese medicine, black sesame nourishes Kidney essence which governs hair. Regular use may support hair health over months.

faqs.q: Should I grind black sesame seeds?
faqs.a: Yes. Whole seeds pass through undigested. Grinding releases the nutrients for better absorption.

faqs.q: Black vs white sesame?
faqs.a: In Chinese medicine, black sesame is preferred for medicinal use as it specifically nourishes Kidney essence.

summary: Brain-shaped nuts that warm the Kidney, strengthen the lower back, and support memory. Also moisten the intestines.
actions: Strengthens the lower back and knees
howToUse: Eat 5-8 walnuts daily, chew thoroughly. Add to oatmeal, congee, or trail mix.
cautions: Avoid if you have damp-heat or oily skin
Too many may cause throat irritation
Not suitable for people with internal Heat
May worsen acne in Damp Heat types

faqs.q: Why do walnuts look like brains?
faqs.a: In Chinese medicine, this visual resemblance is taken seriously. Walnuts are classified as a brain tonic and support memory.

faqs.q: Can walnuts help with lower back pain?
faqs.a: In Chinese medicine, lower back pain from Kidney deficiency may benefit from walnuts.

faqs.q: How many walnuts should I eat daily?
faqs.a: 5-8 walnuts per day is a good amount. Chew thoroughly for best absorption.

summary: A versatile root that simultaneously strengthens three organs: Spleen for digestion, Lung for immunity, and Kidney for reserves.
actions: Supports Lung Qi and immune function
Gently tonifies Kidney without being heavy
howToUse: Peel, slice, and stir-fry with salt and sesame oil. Add to soups and stews. Steam and drizzle with honey.
cautions: Avoid if you have severe dampness or bloating
May cause mild constipation in excess
Not suitable for acute infections
Reduce if you feel overly full

faqs.q: Is Chinese yam the same as sweet potato?
faqs.a: No. Chinese yam (shan yao) is a different root, specifically strengthens the Spleen and Kidney.

faqs.q: Can Chinese yam help with bloating?
faqs.a: Yes. Chinese yam strengthens the Spleen which is responsible for digestion.

faqs.q: Should I cook Chinese yam or eat it raw?
faqs.a: Always cook it. Raw Chinese yam can cause skin irritation and is harder to digest.

summary: Small sweet dried fruit that builds Blood, calms the mind, and supports restful sleep. Called Dragon Eye in English.
actions: Nourishes Heart Blood and calms the spirit
howToUse: Simmer 10-15 dried longan in water for 10 minutes as tea. Add to congee with red dates.
cautions: Not suitable for Damp Heat or phlegm patterns
May cause bloating in excess
Avoid if you have a sore throat or fever
Not for people who run warm internally

faqs.q: What does longan taste like?
faqs.a: Dried longan is sweet and rich, similar to a date but with a floral note.

faqs.q: Does longan help with sleep?
faqs.a: Yes. Longan nourishes Heart Blood which anchors the mind. A tea before bed may help calm racing thoughts.

faqs.q: Longan vs red dates?
faqs.a: They work well together. Red dates strengthen Qi and Blood, while longan specifically calms the mind.

summary: The strongest cooling food in Chinese medicine. Clears internal heat, supports skin health, and is eaten during hot summer months.
actions: Helps the body process excess fluids
howToUse: Simmer 1 cup mung beans in 4 cups water for 30 minutes. Add rock sugar for sweet soup.
cautions: Avoid if you have cold-type digestion or diarrhea
Not suitable for Yang Deficient types
May cause bloating if undercooked
Do not eat raw

faqs.q: Are mung beans good for acne?
faqs.a: Yes. In Chinese medicine, acne often relates to damp-heat. Mung beans clear heat and dampness, which may help skin.

faqs.q: How do I cook mung beans?
faqs.a: Simmer in water for 30 minutes until soft. Add rock sugar for a sweet soup.

faqs.q: Can I eat mung beans every day?
faqs.a: Daily is fine in summer for heat types, but reduce in winter or if you tend to run cold.

summary: The strongest damp-draining food in Chinese medicine. Reduces water retention, supports weight management, and clears heat.
actions: May help with weight management when combined with diet
howToUse: Boil 50g adzuki beans in 3 cups water for 45 minutes. Drink the water or eat the beans.
cautions: Avoid if you have severe cold-type digestion
Not suitable for frequent urination from Yang Deficiency
May cause bloating if not cooked thoroughly
Do not eat raw

faqs.q: Are adzuki beans good for weight loss?
faqs.a: In Chinese medicine, stubborn weight may relate to dampness. Adzuki beans drain dampness and may support healthy weight management.

faqs.q: Adzuki vs mung beans?
faqs.a: Adzuki beans drain dampness and reduce water retention. Mung beans clear heat. They serve different purposes.

faqs.q: How do I cook adzuki beans?
faqs.a: Soak overnight, then boil for 45 minutes until soft. Can be made into paste, soup, or tea.

summary: A grain that drains dampness while strengthening the Spleen. Used for water retention, skin issues, and digestive support.
actions: May help clear skin issues like acne
Gentle daily food for damp types
howToUse: Cook as porridge with rice (1 part coix seed to 3 parts rice). Or boil in water as tea.
cautions: Not suitable during early pregnancy
Avoid if you have severe Yin Deficiency
May cause dry mouth in excess
Cook thoroughly, do not eat raw

faqs.q: Is coix seed the same as barley?
faqs.a: No. Coix seed (Jobs tears) is a different grain from barley. It is specifically used in Chinese medicine for draining dampness.

faqs.q: Can coix seed help with acne?
faqs.a: Yes. Coix seed drains damp-heat which may contribute to acne. It is often combined with mung beans.

faqs.q: How do I cook coix seed?
faqs.a: Soak for 2 hours, then cook as porridge with rice. Or boil in water for 1 hour as tea.

summary: Small ivory seeds that calm the mind, support sleep, and strengthen digestion. A key ingredient in Chinese dessert soups.
actions: Calms the Heart and settles the mind
Strengthens the Spleen and aids digestion
howToUse: Soak dried lotus seeds overnight, then simmer 30 minutes. Add rock sugar for sweet soup.
cautions: Avoid if you have severe constipation
Not suitable for early-stage colds
May cause bloating in excess
Remove the bitter green center before use

faqs.q: Do lotus seeds help with sleep?
faqs.a: Yes. Lotus seeds calm the Heart and settle the mind. They are often combined with lily bulb in sleep-supporting soup.

faqs.q: Where can I buy lotus seeds?
faqs.a: Asian grocery stores, usually in the dried goods section. Also available online.

faqs.q: Do I need to remove the center?
faqs.a: Yes, the green embryo in the center is very bitter. Most dried lotus seeds sold have it removed already.

summary: A pale scaly bulb that nourishes Heart and Lung Yin. Calms the mind, reduces dry cough, and supports restful sleep.
actions: Nourishes Heart Yin and calms the mind
Moistens the Lung and reduces dry cough
howToUse: Simmer 10-15g dried lily bulb with lotus seeds and rock sugar for 30 minutes as sweet soup.
cautions: Not suitable for cold-type cough with clear phlegm
Avoid if you have wind-cold symptoms
May cause loose stools in excess
Use caution during early pregnancy

faqs.q: Is lily bulb the same as the flower?
faqs.a: No. The medicinal lily bulb (bai he) is the fleshy underground bulb, not the flower petals. It is eaten as food in Chinese cuisine.

faqs.q: Does lily bulb help with insomnia?
faqs.a: Yes. Lily bulb nourishes Heart Yin which helps calm the mind for sleep. Combine with lotus seeds for best effect.

faqs.q: Where can I buy lily bulb?
faqs.a: Asian grocery stores in the dried goods section. Look for white, papery, scaly bulbs.

summary: A gelatinous fungus that deeply moistens the body. Used for dry skin, dry cough, and as a natural skin moisturizer from within.
actions: Moistens the Lung and relieves dry cough
howToUse: Soak 10g dried white fungus for 2 hours, tear into pieces, simmer with rock sugar for 1 hour.
dosage: 10-15g dried per serving, 2-3 times per week.
cautions: Avoid if you have cold-type digestion
Not suitable during acute cold with clear phlegm
May cause bloating if not cooked long enough
Do not eat raw

faqs.q: Is white fungus good for skin?
faqs.a: Yes. White fungus is considered a natural moisturizer from within. It generates fluids that may help dry skin.

faqs.q: How do I prepare white fungus?
faqs.a: Soak in cold water for 2 hours until it expands. Tear into small pieces. Simmer with rock sugar for 1 hour until gelatinous.

faqs.q: White fungus vs bird nest?
faqs.a: White fungus is sometimes called the poor mans bird nest because it has similar moisturizing properties at a fraction of the cost.

summary: A refreshing herb that cools the body, moves stuck energy, and opens the sinuses. Used for stress, headaches, and sore throat.
actions: Moves Liver Qi and eases frustration
Opens the sinuses and clears the head
Soothes sore throat when brewed as tea
howToUse: Steep 5-10 fresh leaves or 3-5g dried mint in hot water for 5 minutes. Drink as tea.
dosage: 3-10g dried, or a small handful of fresh leaves daily.
cautions: Avoid if you are always cold or have weak digestion
Not suitable for nursing mothers (may reduce milk)
May cause sweating in excess
Do not use with warming tonics like ginseng

faqs.q: Is mint tea good for stress?
faqs.a: Yes. In Chinese medicine, mint moves Liver Qi which governs emotional flow. It may ease frustration and tension.

faqs.q: Can mint help with headaches?
faqs.a: Yes, especially tension headaches from stress. The cooling and moving action helps release head tension.

faqs.q: Fresh vs dried mint?
faqs.a: Both work. Fresh mint is milder and more aromatic. Dried mint is more concentrated. Use more fresh leaves than dried.

summary: Dried rose petals brewed as tea that moves Liver Qi and eases emotional tension. The classic PMS mood swing remedy.
actions: Moves Liver Qi and releases emotional tension
Eases PMS mood swings and breast tenderness
howToUse: Steep 5-8 dried rose buds in hot water for 5 minutes. Drink 1-2 cups daily, especially before periods.
cautions: Avoid during pregnancy
Not suitable for people with heavy menstrual bleeding
May cause mild stomach upset in excess
Do not use with blood-thinning medications

faqs.q: Is rose tea good for PMS?
faqs.a: Yes. In Chinese medicine, rose petals move Liver Qi which is the root of PMS mood swings and breast tenderness.

faqs.q: Can men drink rose tea?
faqs.a: Yes. Rose tea is not gender-specific. It moves Liver Qi for anyone experiencing stress, frustration, or irritability.

faqs.q: Where can I buy dried rose buds?
faqs.a: Asian grocery stores, tea shops, and online. Look for food-grade dried rose buds, not decorative ones.

summary: A deeply warming bark that warms the Kidney, strengthens Yang, and improves circulation. Used for cold extremities and lower back ache.
actions: Warms Kidney Yang and strengthens reserves
Warms the Spleen and aids digestion
howToUse: Add a small stick to tea, congee, or stew. Use 1-2g powdered in warm milk or oatmeal.
dosage: 1-3g powdered, or 1-2 small sticks per day.
cautions: Avoid if you have internal Heat or Yin Deficiency
Not suitable during pregnancy in medicinal doses
May interact with blood-thinning medications
Do not use if you have hot flashes or night sweats

faqs.q: Is cinnamon the same as cassia?
faqs.a: In Chinese medicine, rou gui is specifically cinnamon bark (cassia bark). It is warmer and more medicinal than common ground cinnamon.

faqs.q: Can cinnamon help with cold hands and feet?
faqs.a: Yes. Cinnamon warms Kidney Yang and improves circulation to the extremities. It is one of the strongest warming herbs.

faqs.q: Can I use regular cinnamon powder?
faqs.a: For cooking yes, but for medicinal purposes, Chinese cinnamon bark (rou gui) is preferred as it is stronger and targets the Kidney.

summary: Dried citrus peel that moves Qi, reduces bloating, and transforms phlegm. The older the peel, the more valuable in Chinese medicine.
actions: Aids digestion of heavy or greasy foods
howToUse: Add 3-5g dried peel to tea, congee, or soup. Simmer for 10 minutes. Can also be ground and added to cooking.
cautions: Avoid if you have Yin Deficiency with dry mouth
Not suitable for people with internal Heat
May cause dryness in excess
Use aged peel, not fresh peel, for best effect

faqs.q: Why is aged tangerine peel better?
faqs.a: In Chinese medicine, the aging process mellows the harshness and enhances the Qi-moving properties. The name chen pi literally means old peel.

faqs.q: Can I make my own chen pi?
faqs.a: Yes. Save tangerine peels, dry them thoroughly in the sun, and store in an airtight container. The longer they age, the better.

faqs.q: Does chen pi help with bloating?
faqs.a: Yes. Chen pi moves Spleen and Stomach Qi, which may relieve bloating and fullness after eating.

summary: A cooling fruit that moistens the Lung, relieves dry cough, and generates fluids.
actions: Moistens the Lung and relieves dry cough
howToUse: Steam with rock sugar for 30 minutes. Slice in tea. Eat fresh in summer.
cautions: Avoid if you have cold-type digestive issues
Not suitable for Yang Deficient types in winter
May cause diarrhea if eaten on empty stomach
Reduce if you have dampness

faqs.q: Should I cook pear or eat it raw?
faqs.a: Cooking pear enhances its moistening effect. Raw pear is fine in summer.

faqs.q: Does pear help with cough?
faqs.a: Yes, for dry cough. Steamed pear with rock sugar is a classic Chinese remedy.

faqs.q: Is pear good for sore throat?
faqs.a: Yes. Pear generates fluids and cools heat, which may help soothe a dry throat.

summary: A mild melon that drains dampness, reduces swelling, and clears heat.
howToUse: Slice and cook in soup for 30-40 minutes. Add to stews.
cautions: Avoid if you have cold-type digestive issues
Not suitable for Yang Deficient types in winter
May cause loose stools if overeaten
Reduce in cold weather

faqs.q: Is winter melon the same as watermelon?
faqs.a: No. Winter melon is a large mild gourd used in savory soups.

faqs.q: Does winter melon help with water retention?
faqs.a: Yes. Winter melon promotes urination and drains dampness.

faqs.q: How do I cook winter melon?
faqs.a: Slice and simmer in soup for 30-40 minutes.

summary: A grain that drains dampness, supports digestion, and clears mild heat. Barley tea is a popular daily drink in East Asia.
howToUse: Roast barley grains and brew as tea. Cook with adzuki beans for damp-draining soup.
cautions: Avoid if you have cold-type digestive issues
Not suitable for Yang Deficient types in winter
May reduce milk supply in nursing mothers
Use in moderation if you have gluten sensitivity

faqs.q: Is barley tea good for dampness?
faqs.a: Yes. Barley tea is one of the gentlest daily drinks for draining dampness.

faqs.q: Does barley tea have caffeine?
faqs.a: No, barley tea is naturally caffeine-free.

faqs.q: Can I drink barley tea every day?
faqs.a: Yes, barley tea is gentle enough for daily use, especially in summer.

summary: A crunchy aquatic root that clears heat, generates fluids, and supports digestion.
howToUse: Slice and stir-fry. Cook in soup for 1 hour. Juice fresh lotus root for heat clearing.
cautions: Avoid if you have cold-type digestive issues
Not suitable for Yang Deficient types in winter
Raw lotus root is very cooling
May cause bloating if undercooked

faqs.q: Can I eat lotus root raw?
faqs.a: Yes, but it is very cooling. Cook it if digestion is weak.

faqs.q: Does lotus root help with nosebleeds?
faqs.a: In Chinese medicine, lotus root clears heat from blood and may help with minor bleeding.

faqs.q: How do I cook lotus root?
faqs.a: Slice and stir-fry with vinegar, or simmer in soup for 1 hour.

summary: A powerful heat-clearing flower used for sore throats, early colds, and skin inflammation.
howToUse: Steep 6-10g dried flowers in hot water for 5 minutes as tea.
cautions: Avoid if you have cold-type digestive issues
Not for long-term daily use
Not suitable for Yang Deficient types
Stop once symptoms improve

faqs.q: Is honeysuckle good for sore throat?
faqs.a: Yes. Honeysuckle clears heat and toxicity, making it effective for sore throat.

faqs.q: Can I drink honeysuckle tea every day?
faqs.a: No. Honeysuckle is cold and meant for short-term use during heat conditions.

faqs.q: When should I take honeysuckle?
faqs.a: At the first sign of a sore throat or heat-type cold. Use for 2-3 days then stop.

summary: The premier Blood tonic in Chinese medicine. Often called female ginseng. Used for menstrual issues and Blood deficiency.
howToUse: Simmer 5-10g in chicken soup for 2 hours. Brew with red dates as tea.
dosage: 5-10g in food. Up to 15g for deficiency.
cautions: Avoid during pregnancy
Not suitable during heavy menstrual bleeding
May increase bleeding risk, stop before surgery
Avoid if you have diarrhea or bloating

faqs.q: Is dong quai good for periods?
faqs.a: In Chinese medicine, dong quai nourishes and regulates Blood. Always consult a practitioner.

faqs.q: Can I take dong quai during pregnancy?
faqs.a: No. Dong quai is contraindicated during pregnancy.

faqs.q: Is dong quai the same as ginseng?
faqs.a: No. Dong quai is a Blood tonic, while ginseng is a Qi tonic.

summary: A root that nourishes Kidney and Liver Yin, clears heat, and builds Blood.
actions: May help with hot flashes and night sweats
howToUse: Usually used in herbal formulas. Prepared rehmannia can be added to soups.
cautions: Avoid if you have dampness or bloating
May cause loose stools
Not suitable for Yang Deficient types
Consult a practitioner for proper use

faqs.q: What is the difference between raw and prepared rehmannia?
faqs.a: Raw rehmannia is cooling and clears heat. Prepared rehmannia is warmer and builds Blood and Yin.

faqs.q: Is rehmannia good for menopause?
faqs.a: In Chinese medicine, rehmannia nourishes Kidney Yin which declines during menopause.

faqs.q: Can I cook with rehmannia at home?
faqs.a: Prepared rehmannia can be added to soups. Raw rehmannia is usually used in formulas.

summary: A medicinal mushroom that drains dampness, strengthens the Spleen, and calms the mind.
actions: Calms the mind and supports sleep
Gentle and suitable for long-term use
howToUse: Cook 10-15g in soup or congee. Often combined with red dates and Chinese yam.
cautions: Avoid if you have Yin Deficiency with dryness
May worsen frequent urination
Not suitable for very dry constitution
Generally very safe

faqs.q: What is poria?
faqs.a: Poria (fu ling) is a medicinal fungus that grows on pine tree roots.

faqs.q: Does poria help with sleep?
faqs.a: Yes. Poria calms the mind and is often included in sleep-supporting formulas.

faqs.q: Can I use poria every day?
faqs.a: Yes. Poria is neutral and gentle, suitable for regular use.

summary: Warming seeds that dispel cold, reduce gas, and support digestion. Common kitchen spice.
actions: Disperses cold and warms the middle
May ease menstrual cramps from cold
howToUse: Brew 3-5g seeds as tea. Add to soups and stews. Use as spice in cooking.
cautions: Avoid if you have internal Heat or Yin Deficiency
May worsen hot flashes
Not suitable for Damp Heat types
Use in moderation

faqs.q: Can fennel help with bloating?
faqs.a: Yes. Fennel reduces gas and bloating by warming the digestive system.

faqs.q: Is fennel good for menstrual cramps?
faqs.a: In Chinese medicine, fennel warms the lower abdomen and may help with cold-type cramps.

faqs.q: Can I use kitchen fennel seeds?
faqs.a: Yes. The fennel seeds in your spice cabinet are the same herb used in Chinese medicine.

summary: Unrefined sugar that warms, builds Blood, and eases menstrual cramps. Used in traditional Chinese remedies.
howToUse: Dissolve 1-2 tablespoons in hot ginger tea. Add to red date tea. Use in cooking instead of white sugar.
cautions: Avoid if you have damp-heat
Not suitable for diabetes
May worsen acne in excess
Use in moderation

faqs.q: Is brown sugar better than white sugar?
faqs.a: In Chinese medicine, brown sugar (unrefined) is warming and builds Blood, while white sugar is considered empty sweetness.

faqs.q: Does brown sugar help with period cramps?
faqs.a: Yes. Ginger brown sugar tea is the most common Chinese remedy for menstrual cramps.

faqs.q: Can I use brown sugar every day?
faqs.a: In small amounts (10-15g) it is fine, especially in tea. Avoid excess if you have damp-heat.
